verses.commentary: We who are strong ought to bear with the failings of the weak and not to please ourselves. The strong (those with clear conscience on disputable matters) have an obligation. Bear with (bastazō, βαστάζω) means carry, endure. Failings (asthenēmata, ἀσθενήματα) are weaknesses, scruples. Not pleasing ourselves: self-denial for others' sake. Strength is for service, not self-indulgence.

Each of us should please our neighbors for their good, to build them up. Shift from self to neighbor. Please (areskō, ἀρέσκω) the neighbor. The purpose: their good and building up (oikodomē, οἰκοδομή, edification). Love seeks the other's benefit, not one's own preferences.

For even Christ did not please Himself but, as it is written: The insults of those who insult you have fallen on me (Psalm 69:9). Christ is the supreme example. He did not please Himself. He bore reproach for God's sake. Insults directed at God fell on Christ. If Christ denied Himself, how much more should we.

For everything that was written in the past was written to teach us, so that through the endurance taught in the Scriptures and the encouragement they provide we might have hope. Scripture's purpose: our instruction. Endurance (hypomonē, ὑπομονή) and encouragement (paraklēsis, παράκλησις) from Scripture produce hope. The Old Testament is for our benefit. Scripture sustains hope through trials.

May the God who gives endurance and encouragement give you the same attitude of mind toward each other that Christ Jesus had. A prayer: God grants endurance and encouragement. The petition: unity of mind (to auto phroneō, τὸ αὐτὸ φρονέω) according to Christ Jesus. Christ's mindset (humility, self-denial) should mark believers. Unity flows from shared Christlikeness.

So that with one mind and one voice you may glorify the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ. The goal: united worship. One mind (homothumadon, ὁμοθυμαδόν, with one accord) and one voice (one mouth) glorify God. Unity magnifies God's glory. Divisions hinder worship.

Accept one another, then, just as Christ accepted you, in order to bring praise to God. The command: mutual acceptance (proslambanō, προσλαμβάνω, receive warmly). The ground: Christ accepted us. The purpose: God's glory. Christ's acceptance of us, while we were sinners, models how we accept others.

For I tell you that Christ has become a servant of the Jews on behalf of God's truth, so that the promises made to the patriarchs might be confirmed. Christ's mission to Jews: servant (diakonos, διάκονος) on behalf of God's truth. Purpose: confirm the promises to Abraham, Isaac, Jacob. God's faithfulness to Israel is demonstrated in Christ. The Messiah fulfills covenant promises.

And, moreover, that the Gentiles might glorify God for His mercy. As it is written: Therefore I will praise you among the Gentiles; I will sing the praises of your name (Psalm 18:49). Christ's mission also includes Gentiles. They glorify God for His mercy (eleos, ἔλεος). Old Testament prophecy (Psalm 18:49) foretold Gentile worship. The gospel extends beyond Israel to all nations.

Again, it says, Rejoice, you Gentiles, with His people (Deuteronomy 32:43). A second quotation. Gentiles rejoice with God's people (Israel). This anticipates the unity of Jew and Gentile in one body. The Old Testament envisioned this inclusion.

And again, Praise the Lord, all you Gentiles; let all the peoples extol Him (Psalm 117:1). A third quotation. All Gentiles are called to praise. All peoples (panta ta ethnē, πάντα τὰ ἔθνη) extol God. This is universal worship. God's glory extends to every nation.

And again, Isaiah says, The Root of Jesse will spring up, one who will arise to rule over the nations; in Him the Gentiles will hope (Isaiah 11:10). A fourth quotation. The Root of Jesse (Messiah from David's line) will rule the nations (Gentiles). Gentiles will hope in Him. Christ is the fulfillment: King over all nations, hope for all peoples.

May the God of hope fill you with all joy and peace as you trust in Him, so that you may overflow with hope by the power of the Holy Spirit. A benediction. God of hope grants joy and peace through trust. The result: overflowing hope by the Spirit's power. Hope, joy, and peace are Spirit-given realities. Trust in God produces abundant hope.

I myself am convinced, my brothers and sisters, that you yourselves are full of goodness, filled with knowledge and competent to instruct one another. Paul's confidence in the Romans. Full of goodness (agathōsynē, ἀγαθωσύνη), filled with knowledge (gnōsis, γνῶσις), and competent to instruct (noutheteō, νουθετέω, admonish). This is pastoral affirmation. Believers can teach and correct each other.

Yet I have written you quite boldly on some points to remind you of them again, because of the grace God gave me. Paul wrote boldly (tolmēroterōs, τολμηρότερως, more daringly) as a reminder. The basis: grace given him (apostolic calling). Paul's boldness flows from his commission, not arrogance.

To be a minister of Christ Jesus to the Gentiles. He gave me the priestly duty of proclaiming the gospel of God, so that the Gentiles might become an offering acceptable to God, sanctified by the Holy Spirit. Paul's role: minister (leitourgos, λειτουργός, public servant) to Gentiles. Priestly duty (hierourgeō, ἱερουργέω, priestly service) of proclaiming the gospel. The Gentiles are an offering (prosphora, προσφορά) to God, sanctified by the Spirit. Paul's ministry is priestly: presenting converted Gentiles as a sacrifice to God.

Therefore I glory in Christ Jesus in my service to God. Paul's boasting (kauchēsis, καύχησις) is in Christ Jesus, not himself. His service (ta pros ton theon, τὰ πρὸς τὸν θεόν, things pertaining to God) is through Christ. All ministry glory belongs to Christ.

I will not venture to speak of anything except what Christ has accomplished through me in leading the Gentiles to obey God by what I have said and done. Paul limits his boasting to what Christ accomplished through him. Leading Gentiles to obedience (hypakoē, ὑπακοή) is the goal. Word and deed (speech and action) both matter. Ministry success is Christ's work, not Paul's.

By the power of signs and wonders, through the power of the Spirit of God. So from Jerusalem all the way around to Illyricum, I have fully proclaimed the gospel of Christ. Signs and wonders (sēmeia kai terata, σημεῖα καὶ τέρατα) authenticated Paul's message. Spirit's power enabled ministry. Geographic scope: Jerusalem to Illyricum (modern Balkans). Fully proclaimed (plēroō, πληρόω) means completed, fulfilled. Paul's missionary reach was vast.

It has always been my ambition to preach the gospel where Christ was not known, so that I would not be building on someone else's foundation. Paul's ambition (philotimeomai, φιλοτιμέομαι, aspire, make it one's aim): pioneer evangelism. Preach where Christ is not known. Not building on another's foundation respects others' work and extends gospel reach. Paul was a church planter, not a settler.

Rather, as it is written: Those who were not told about Him will see, and those who have not heard will understand (Isaiah 52:15). Old Testament warrant for Paul's mission. Those not told will see; those not hearing will understand. This justifies reaching unreached peoples. God's plan includes taking the gospel to those who have never heard.

This is why I have often been hindered from coming to you. Paul's pioneer focus delayed his Rome visit. His calling to unreached areas kept him from visiting established churches. Priorities shaped his travel.

But now that there is no more place for me to work in these regions, and since I have been longing for many years to visit you. The eastern Mediterranean is evangelized (not exhaustively, but foundationally). No more place means no more pioneering opportunities in that region. Paul's longstanding desire: visit Rome. Timing and calling align.

I plan to do so when I go to Spain. I hope to see you while passing through and to have you assist me on my journey there, after I have enjoyed your company for a while. Paul's plan: Spain mission, with Rome as a stopover. Assist (propempō, προπέμπω) means send on one's way with provision. Paul hopes for Roman support for Spain. After enjoying fellowship, he will continue west. Fellowship and mission are both priorities.

Now, however, I am on my way to Jerusalem in the service of the Lord's people there. Immediate plan: Jerusalem with a collection for needy believers. Service (diakoneō, διακονέω, minister) to the Lord's people. This refers to the collection from Gentile churches for Jewish believers (Acts 24:17). Unity is expressed through generosity.

For Macedonia and Achaia were pleased to make a contribution for the poor among the Lord's people in Jerusalem. Macedonia (Philippi, Thessalonica, Berea) and Achaia (Corinth) contributed to the collection. Pleased (eudokeō, εὐδοκέω) indicates willing generosity. The poor (ptōchoi, πτωχοί) in Jerusalem needed support. Gentile believers helped Jewish believers.

They were pleased to do it, and indeed they owe it to them. For if the Gentiles have shared in the Jews' spiritual blessings, they owe it to the Jews to share with them their material blessings. Obligation (opheilō, ὀφείλω, owe) motivates giving. Gentiles received spiritual blessings (the gospel) from Jews. Jews should receive material blessings from Gentiles. This reciprocal relationship expresses unity.

So after I have completed this task and have made sure that they have received this contribution, I will go to Spain and visit you on the way. Paul's itinerary: complete the collection delivery, then Spain via Rome. Made sure (sphragizō, σφραγίζω, sealed, secured) suggests safe delivery. Paul's plans are detailed and purposeful.

I know that when I come to you, I will come in the full measure of the blessing of Christ. Paul's expectation: his visit will bring blessing (eulogia, εὐλογία). Full measure (plērōma, πλήρωμα) suggests abundant blessing. Apostolic presence brings Christ's blessing to the church.

I urge you, brothers and sisters, by our Lord Jesus Christ and by the love of the Spirit, to join me in my struggle by praying to God for me. A plea for prayer. By Christ and by the Spirit's love, Paul urges prayer. Join in my struggle (synagōnizomai, συναγωνίζομαι, strive together with) depicts partnership. Prayer is spiritual warfare. Paul needs the Romans' intercession.

Pray that I may be kept safe from the unbelievers in Judea and that the contribution I take to Jerusalem may be favorably received by the Lord's people there. Two prayer requests: safety from unbelievers (Jews who opposed Paul) and favorable reception of the collection by Jerusalem believers. Both are real concerns. Not all Jewish believers trusted Paul or welcomed Gentile support.

So that I may come to you with joy, by God's will, and in your company be refreshed. The goal: joyful arrival in Rome by God's will. Be refreshed (synanapauomai, συναναπαύομαι, rest together with) suggests mutual encouragement. Paul longs for fellowship with the Romans. Ministry is sustained by Christian community.
The God of peace be with you all. Amen. A benediction. The God of peace (eirēnē, εἰρήνη, shalom) grants wholeness. With you all is comprehensive. Amen (so be it) seals the blessing.
